_meta.scope: Citation-grade replacements for generated Soldier's Story representative rows.
_meta.note: D294 adds the twenty-seventh narrow sourced replacement slice: John Camden West, a Private of Company E, 4th Texas Infantry, Robertson's Texas Brigade, Hood's Division (Law commanding) at Chickamauga, replacing the open Law/Hood division private slot beside D291's Val C. Giles NCO row. D293 added John S. Jackman as the twenty-sixth record; D291 added Giles, Chambers, and Houston; D290 added Benson, Green, and Tunnard; D289 added Stanley, Dooley, and De Castro. Side balance after D294 is 14US/13CS. Each record must still pass tools/import-soldier-replacements.mjs, the build gate, the focused browser probe, and the normal focused gate. Template values below are documentation only and are ignored by the validator.

records.name: Elisha Hunt Rhodes

sources.title: Elisha Hunt Rhodes Papers, 1858-1904
sources.author: Rhode Island Historical Society
sources.note: Finding aid for Rhodes's papers; states he enlisted as a private in the 2nd Rhode Island Infantry on June 5, 1861 and served at First Bull Run.

sources.title: Elisha H. Rhodes
sources.author: Rhode Island Heritage Hall of Fame
sources.repository: Rhode Island Heritage Hall of Fame
sources.note: States Rhodes enlisted as a private in Company D, 2nd Rhode Island, and fought at Bull Run before later promotion.

sources.title: 2nd Regiment, Rhode Island Infantry
sources.author: National Park Service
sources.repository: Civil War Soldiers and Sailors System
sources.note: Places the 2nd Rhode Island in Burnside's Brigade, Hunter's Division, McDowell's Army, with Bull Run service on July 21, 1861.

sources.title: Elisha Hunt Rhodes
sources.author: H. C. Foster
sources.repository: Library of Congress Prints and Photographs Division
sources.note: Wartime portrait source; metadata has no known restrictions but depicts Rhodes as a later officer, not as a Bull Run private.

records.sourceNote: RIHS verifies Rhodes enlisted as a private in the 2nd Rhode Island and served Bull Run; RI Heritage adds Company D; NPS places the regiment in Burnside's Brigade/Hunter's Division at Bull Run. Ratings stay neutral Inferred game calibration.
records.bio: Elisha Hunt Rhodes was nineteen when he enlisted in Company D, 2nd Rhode Island Infantry, in June 1861. He fought at First Bull Run as a private in Burnside's Brigade, part of Hunter's Division. Rhodes kept wartime journals and letters and later rose through the regiment's ranks, eventually commanding it as colonel. This replacement uses only the verified 1861 claim: a private of the 2nd Rhode Island on the Bull Run field.

portrait.alt: Wartime portrait of Elisha Hunt Rhodes in Union officer uniform, photographed after his Bull Run private service.
portrait.caption: Elisha Hunt Rhodes in a later wartime officer portrait; he served at First Bull Run as a private.
portrait.credit: Library of Congress Prints and Photographs Division

records.name: William McCarter

sources.title: My Life in the Irish Brigade: The Civil War Memoirs of Private William McCarter, 116th Pennsylvania Infantry
sources.author: William McCarter
sources.locator: Published Materials catalog; 1996 Da Capo edition
sources.note: Cataloged memoir title identifies McCarter as a private in the 116th Pennsylvania Infantry.

sources.title: Philadelphia William McCarter documented his military service with the 116th Pennsylvania Infantry Regiment during the Civil War
sources.author: Cary Hutto
sources.note: States McCarter joined the 116th Pennsylvania in August 1862 and fought at Fredericksburg.

sources.title: The Medical Treatment of Private William McCarter
sources.author: Catherine Bateson
sources.repository: National Museum of Civil War Medicine
sources.note: Identifies McCarter as Private William McCarter, enlisted in the 116th Pennsylvania, wounded at Fredericksburg, and tied to the Irish Brigade.

sources.title: Order of Battle Fredericksburg Union Right Grand Division
sources.author: National Park Service
sources.repository: Fredericksburg and Spotsylvania National Military Park
sources.locator: Second Army Corps / First Division / Second Brigade (Irish Brigade)
sources.note: Places the 116th Pennsylvania in Thomas Meagher's Irish Brigade, First Division, II Corps at Fredericksburg.

records.sourceNote: HSP and NMCWM identify McCarter as a private of the 116th Pennsylvania and place his Fredericksburg service/wounding in the Irish Brigade; NPS places the 116th Pennsylvania in Meagher's brigade, First Division, II Corps. No company or portrait is asserted. Ratings stay neutral Inferred calibration.
records.bio: William McCarter, an Irish immigrant living in Philadelphia, joined the 116th Pennsylvania Infantry in August 1862. At Fredericksburg he served as a private connected to Meagher's Irish Brigade and was badly wounded during the assault near Marye's Heights. His later memoir, My Life in the Irish Brigade, makes this replacement a narrow sourced fit for the generated Fredericksburg Irish Brigade private slot; the record does not assert a company or portrait.

records.name: Sam R. Watkins

sources.title: Sam Watkins
sources.author: National Park Service
sources.repository: Chickamauga and Chattanooga National Military Park
sources.note: Identifies Watkins as a private in Company H, First Tennessee Infantry, states the First Tennessee fought both days at Chickamauga, and cautions that his memoir omits slavery from the war narrative.

sources.title: "Co. Aytch," Maury Grays, First Tennessee Regiment; Or, A Side Show of the Big Show
sources.author: Samuel R. Watkins
sources.note: Watkins's memoir/title identifies the First Tennessee Regiment and records his Company H and Chickamauga experience from a participant's perspective.

sources.title: 1st/27th Consolidated Regiment, Tennessee Infantry
sources.author: National Park Service
sources.repository: Civil War Soldiers and Sailors System
sources.note: States the 1st Tennessee was consolidated with the 27th after December 1862, assigned to Maney's Brigade, Army of Tennessee, and participated at Chickamauga.

records.sourceNote: NPS identifies Watkins as a private in Company H, First Tennessee Infantry, and places him and the First Tennessee at Chickamauga. Project Gutenberg provides Watkins's memoir trail. NPS unit details place the consolidated 1st/27th Tennessee in Maney's Brigade, Army of Tennessee, for the battlefield context. The modeled game slot is Cheatham's Division, Polk's Wing; no portrait is asserted. Ratings stay neutral Inferred calibration.
records.bio: Sam R. Watkins served as a private in Company H, First Tennessee Infantry, and fought at Chickamauga with the Army of Tennessee. By the 1863 battle the 1st Tennessee had been consolidated with the 27th and served in Maney's Brigade, fitting the modeled Cheatham's Division private slot. Watkins later wrote Co. Aytch, a vivid participant memoir that the NPS also flags for its omission of slavery and enslaved labor from the war story.

records.name: Joshua L. Chamberlain

sources.title: The Army of the Potomac at Gettysburg
sources.author: National Park Service
sources.locator: V Corps / First Division / Third Brigade order of battle
sources.note: Places the 20th Maine Infantry under Col. Joshua L. Chamberlain in Strong Vincent's Third Brigade, First Division, V Corps at Gettysburg.

sources.title: 20th Maine Infantry Regiment Monument
sources.author: National Park Service
sources.locator: Little Round Top monument place page
sources.note: States the 20th Maine was commanded by Col. Joshua L. Chamberlain, ordered to the extreme left of the Union line on Little Round Top on July 2, 1863, and charged downhill with bayonets after running low on ammunition.

sources.title: The Civil War - 20th Regiment, Maine Infantry
sources.author: National Park Service
sources.repository: Civil War Soldiers and Sailors System
sources.note: Places the 20th Maine Infantry in the Gettysburg campaign and Battle of Gettysburg, July 1-3, 1863.

sources.title: Defense of Little Round Top
sources.author: American Battlefield Trust
sources.note: Corroborates Chamberlain leading the 20th Maine at Little Round Top and the famous counterattack in the broader defense of the hill.

records.sourceNote: NPS order-of-battle data places Col. Joshua L. Chamberlain and the 20th Maine in Vincent's Third Brigade, First Division, V Corps at Gettysburg. NPS Little Round Top material and the American Battlefield Trust corroborate the regiment's extreme-left position, low-ammunition crisis, and bayonet charge. The record asserts no company and no portrait; ratings stay neutral Inferred calibration.
records.bio: Joshua L. Chamberlain commanded the 20th Maine on Little Round Top during the second day at Gettysburg. The regiment held the extreme left of the Union line in Vincent's Brigade, ran low on ammunition, and counterattacked with the famous bayonet charge. This replacement presents Chamberlain as a sourced regimental commander while preserving the scenario's broader caution that Little Round Top was held by a chain of decisions and costs, not by one man alone.

records.name: Alonzo H. Cushing

sources.title: Alonzo Cushing at Gettysburg
sources.author: National Park Service
sources.note: Places six 3-inch Ordnance Rifles of Battery A, 4th United States Artillery at the Angle on Cemetery Ridge and identifies 1st Lt. Alonzo Cushing as the battery commander.

sources.title: First Lieutenant Alonzo H. Cushing
sources.author: U.S. Army
sources.note: Identifies Cushing as a first lieutenant, artillery branch, commander of Battery A, 4th U.S. Artillery, II Corps, Army of the Potomac, and states he was killed in action on July 3, 1863.

sources.title: Alonzo H Cushing
sources.author: Congressional Medal of Honor Society
sources.note: Gives Cushing's rank as first lieutenant, unit as Battery A, 4th U.S. Artillery, action date July 3, 1863, action place Gettysburg, and Medal of Honor citation for his battery command.

records.sourceNote: NPS places Battery A, 4th U.S. Artillery at the Angle on Cemetery Ridge and identifies 1st Lt. Alonzo Cushing as its commander. Army.mil and the Congressional Medal of Honor Society corroborate his rank, Battery A / 4th U.S. Artillery / II Corps context, July 3 Gettysburg action, and death while directing the guns. The record asserts no portrait; ratings stay neutral Inferred calibration.
records.bio: Alonzo H. Cushing commanded Battery A, 4th U.S. Artillery at the Angle on Cemetery Ridge during Pickett's Charge. On July 3, 1863, his battery was battered by the Confederate cannonade; Cushing was wounded more than once, refused evacuation, and kept directing his remaining guns until he was killed. This replacement ties the Gettysburg artillery slot to a sourced battery commander without changing the battle's shared credit for the Union defense.

records.name: Strong Vincent

sources.title: The Army of the Potomac at Gettysburg
sources.author: National Park Service
sources.locator: V Corps / First Division / Third Brigade order of battle
sources.note: Places Col. Strong Vincent as wounded commander of the Third Brigade, First Division, V Corps at Gettysburg, with the 20th Maine, 83rd Pennsylvania, 44th New York, and 16th Michigan under that brigade.

sources.title: The Memory of Strong Vincent
sources.author: National Park Service
sources.note: Identifies Vincent's Little Round Top action, mortal wounding on July 2, 1863, and death on July 7, 1863.

sources.title: Defense of Little Round Top
sources.author: American Battlefield Trust
sources.note: Corroborates Vincent's brigade arriving on Little Round Top and frames Chamberlain's 20th Maine within the broader brigade defense.

records.sourceNote: NPS OOB places Col. Strong Vincent as commander of the Third Brigade, First Division, V Corps at Gettysburg; NPS/ABT corroborate his Little Round Top action, mortal wound, and July 7 death. This record asserts no company, no regiment command, no brigadier-rank-at-battle, and no portrait. Ratings stay neutral Inferred calibration.
records.bio: Strong Vincent commanded the brigade that hurried onto Little Round Top on July 2, 1863, just before Hood's division struck the Union left. His line included the 16th Michigan, 44th New York, 83rd Pennsylvania, and the 20th Maine. Vincent was mortally wounded while directing the brigade and died on July 7. This replacement makes the brigade-level decision visible while keeping Chamberlain's 20th Maine inside a larger chain of command and cost.

records.name: Leander Stillwell

sources.title: The Story of a Common Soldier of Army Life in the Civil War, 1861-1865
sources.author: Leander Stillwell
sources.locator: EBook #26561, In the Ranks at Shiloh
sources.note: Stillwell's memoir identifies the 61st Illinois and gives his first-person Shiloh account from Prentiss's line.

sources.title: 61st Regiment, Illinois Infantry
sources.author: National Park Service
sources.repository: Civil War Soldiers and Sailors System
sources.note: Places the 61st Illinois at the Battle of Shiloh, April 6-7, 1862, and later Vicksburg service.

sources.title: Roster of Company D, 61st Illinois Infantry
sources.author: Illinois GenWeb Project
sources.note: Lists Leander Stillwell as a private in Company D, 61st Illinois, mustered February 5, 1862, with later promotions.

sources.title: Leander Stillwell
sources.author: History of Allen and Woodson Counties, Kansas
sources.note: States Stillwell enlisted as a private in Company D, 61st Illinois, and participated in Shiloh and Vicksburg.

records.sourceNote: Stillwell's memoir places his 61st Illinois regiment on Prentiss's Shiloh line; NPS corroborates the regiment's Shiloh service. ILGenWeb and the Kansas biography verify Company D/private muster and later promotions. Stillwell's own text supports the corporal/NCO story, while no higher rank and no portrait are asserted. Ratings stay neutral Inferred calibration.
records.bio: Leander Stillwell was an eighteen-year-old from Jersey County, Illinois, when he entered Company D, 61st Illinois Infantry. At Shiloh his regiment stood on the left of Prentiss's line, then was shifted out of the Hornets' Nest fight before the division was surrounded. His later memoir, The Story of a Common Soldier, makes this replacement a deliberately ground-level Shiloh story: the battle as an enlisted man saw it, not a generals-only account.

records.name: John Cook

sources.title: John Cook
sources.author: Congressional Medal of Honor Society
sources.locator: Recipient profile, Civil War / U.S. Army
sources.note: Gives Cook's rank as Bugler, unit as Battery B, 4th U.S. Artillery, action date September 17, 1862, action place Antietam, and Medal of Honor citation.

sources.title: Medal of Honor Monday: Army Bugler John Cook
sources.author: Katie Lange
sources.repository: U.S. Department of War / DOD News
sources.note: Identifies Cook as a Battery B bugler and summarizes his Antietam actions after Capt. Joseph B. Campbell was wounded.

sources.title: Artillery at Antietam
sources.author: National Park Service
sources.locator: Battery B, 4th U.S. Artillery map entry
sources.note: Places Battery B, 4th U.S. Artillery at Antietam under Capt. Joseph B. Campbell, notes six Napoleons, Campbell wounded, heavy cannoneer casualties, and two Medals of Honor from the battery.

sources.title: Antietam Staff Ride and Battlefield Tour
sources.author: U.S. Army Center of Military History
sources.repository: U.S. Army Center of Military History
sources.locator: Order of battle, I Corps / 1st Division / Artillery
sources.note: Places 4th U.S. Artillery, Battery B in the Army of the Potomac, I Corps, First Division artillery order of battle at Antietam.

records.sourceNote: CMOHS verifies Cook's Bugler rank, Battery B / 4th U.S. Artillery unit, and Antietam action date/place. The Department of War account and NPS artillery brochure support the bugler-to-cannoneer story and Battery B's fight after Campbell was wounded; CMH places Battery B in I Corps / First Division. The record asserts no higher rank and no portrait asset. Ratings stay neutral Inferred calibration.
records.bio: John Cook was a teenage bugler in Battery B, 4th U.S. Artillery. At Antietam, after Capt. Joseph B. Campbell was wounded and cannoneers fell around the battery, Cook carried messages and then served a gun as an acting cannoneer. This replacement keeps him as a Battery B artilleryman and adds a documented youth/bugler-to-gunner perspective to the Soldier's Story lane without changing the battle model.

records.name: Orion P. Howe

sources.title: Orion P Howe
sources.author: Congressional Medal of Honor Society
sources.locator: Recipient profile, Civil War / U.S. Army
sources.note: Gives Howe's rank as Musician, Company C / 55th Illinois Infantry unit, May 19, 1863 Vicksburg action date/place, and Medal of Honor citation.

sources.title: Letter from General William T. Sherman concerning Orion Howe of Waukegan (1863)
sources.author: William T. Sherman
sources.locator: Military and Naval Department Record Series 301.018; online exhibit document 39
sources.note: Sherman's letter trail and archive background identify the 14-year-old Howe, his wound, and his cartridge message to Sherman during the Vicksburg fighting.

sources.title: 55th Illinois Infantry
sources.author: National Park Service
sources.note: Places the 55th Illinois at the May 19 assault near the Stockade Redan and identifies its attachment to Blair's Second Division, Sherman's XV Corps, under Col. Oscar Malmborg.

sources.title: 55th Regiment, Illinois Infantry
sources.author: National Park Service
sources.repository: Civil War Soldiers and Sailors System
sources.note: Confirms the 55th Illinois served in the Vicksburg siege and the May 19 and May 22 assaults.

sources.title: The Campaign for Vicksburg
sources.author: National Park Service
sources.note: Places Blair's division in Sherman's May 19 assault against the Stockade Redan and describes the failed Graveyard Road attack.

records.sourceNote: CMOHS verifies Howe's Musician rank, Company C / 55th Illinois, and May 19 Vicksburg action. Illinois State Archives supports the wounded drummer/cartridge-message story; NPS places the 55th Illinois in Blair's Second Division, XV Corps. This record asserts no higher rank at Vicksburg and no portrait asset. Ratings stay neutral Inferred calibration.
records.bio: Orion P. Howe was a fourteen-year-old musician in Company C, 55th Illinois Infantry. During the May 19, 1863 assault at Vicksburg, he was wounded while carrying a cartridge message to William T. Sherman for Col. Oscar Malmborg's men. This replacement ties the Blair's Division generated row to a documented drummer/ammunition-runner story and keeps the Vicksburg lesson on courage inside a failed assault, not a romantic breakthrough.

records.name: Francis A. Waller

sources.title: Civil War (M - Z Index) Medal of Honor recipients
sources.author: The United States Army
sources.note: Verifies Waller's Corporal rank, Company I / 6th Wisconsin unit, Gettysburg July 1 action, and citation for capturing the 2d Mississippi flag.

sources.title: Faces of Gettysburg - Francis Ashbury Wallar - Medal of Honor Winner
sources.author: D. Scott Hartwig
sources.note: Uses the Wallar spelling; describes the Railroad Cut charge, flag capture, promotion from corporal to sergeant, and later memory dispute.

sources.title: The Army of the Potomac at Gettysburg
sources.author: National Park Service
sources.locator: First Corps / First Division / First Brigade (Iron Brigade)
sources.note: Places the 6th Wisconsin Infantry under Lt. Col. Rufus R. Dawes in the First Brigade (Iron Brigade), First Division, I Corps.

records.sourceNote: Army.mil verifies Francis A. Waller's Corporal rank, Company I / 6th Wisconsin unit, and Gettysburg July 1 citation. NPS Gettysburg, using the Wallar spelling, supports the Railroad Cut flag-capture story and memory caveat; NPS OOB places the regiment in the Iron Brigade. The record asserts no higher rank at Gettysburg and no portrait asset.
records.bio: Francis A. Waller, spelled Wallar by Gettysburg National Military Park, was a corporal in Company I, 6th Wisconsin Infantry. On July 1, 1863, during the Railroad Cut fighting at Gettysburg, he was credited with capturing the 2nd Mississippi Infantry flag and was promoted to sergeant for conspicuous bravery. This replacement keeps the story in the Iron Brigade NCO row while noting that later veterans disputed parts of the memory trail.

records.name: Samuel N. Benjamin

sources.title: Civil War (A - L Index) Medal of Honor recipients
sources.author: The United States Army
sources.note: Verifies Benjamin's First Lieutenant rank, 2d U.S. Artillery organization, and Medal of Honor citation for distinguished artillery service.

sources.title: Samuel Nicholl Benjamin
sources.author: Congressional Medal of Honor Society
sources.note: Identifies Benjamin as a U.S. Army First Lieutenant in the 2d U.S. Artillery and gives the long-service citation and later brevet rank caveat.

sources.title: Army of the Potomac - Ninth Corps
sources.author: National Park Service
sources.locator: First Division / Artillery order of battle
sources.note: Places Battery E, 2nd U.S. Artillery under Lt. Samuel N. Benjamin in the IX Corps order of battle.

sources.title: Lt Samuel N Benjamin's Official Reports
sources.author: Samuel N. Benjamin
sources.repository: Antietam on the Web / Official Records transcription
sources.note: Transcribes Benjamin's report for Battery E, Second Artillery operations near Sharpsburg on September 16-17, 1862.

records.sourceNote: Army.mil and CMOHS verify Samuel N. Benjamin's First Lieutenant rank, 2d U.S. Artillery unit, and long-service Medal of Honor citation. NPS Antietam places Battery E, 2nd U.S., under Lt. Benjamin in IX Corps; his transcribed report anchors the Sept. 16-17 battery story. The record asserts no captain-rank-at-Antietam and no portrait asset.
records.bio: Samuel N. Benjamin commanded Battery E, 2nd U.S. Artillery in the Maryland Campaign as a first lieutenant. At Antietam, his report described the battery shifting near the Stone Bridge, firing on Confederate batteries around Sharpsburg, and using its last six rounds late in the day. This replacement turns the generated Antietam Benjamin artillery slot into a sourced IX Corps battery-commander story without asserting his later captaincy or any portrait.

records.name: Francis C. Barlow

sources.title: Francis Barlow: Rising Through the Ranks
sources.author: National Park Service
sources.repository: Fredericksburg and Spotsylvania National Military Park
sources.note: Identifies Barlow as Col. Barlow at Antietam, places him at Bloody Lane, and notes his severe wound before later general-officer service.

sources.title: Army of the Potomac - Second Corps
sources.author: National Park Service
sources.locator: First Division / Caldwell's First Brigade order of battle
sources.note: Places the 61st and 64th New York under Col. Francis C. Barlow in Caldwell's Brigade, First Division, II Corps.

sources.title: Col Francis C Barlow's Official Report
sources.author: Francis C. Barlow
sources.repository: Antietam on the Web / Official Records transcription
sources.locator: Official Records, Series 1, Vol. 19, Part 1, pages 289-290
sources.note: Barlow reports commanding the 61st and 64th New York, gaining enfilading fire on the Sunken Road, taking prisoners, and being wounded.

sources.title: Col Francis Channing Barlow
sources.author: Antietam on the Web
sources.note: Summarizes Barlow's 61st/64th New York command, Caldwell brigade context, Sunken Road flank attack, prisoner capture, and wound.

records.sourceNote: NPS verifies Francis C. Barlow as Colonel at Antietam and places the 61st/64th New York under him in Caldwell's Brigade, First Division, II Corps. Barlow's Official Records report anchors the enfilading-fire and prisoner-capture story. The record asserts no brigadier-rank-at-Antietam, no single-company claim, and no portrait asset.
records.bio: Francis C. Barlow commanded the 61st and 64th New York at Antietam as a colonel in Caldwell's Brigade. In his report he described using the regiments' position to fire into the flank of Confederate troops in the Sunken Road, helping break the Bloody Lane position before he was wounded later in the fight. This replacement keeps the story on the Antietam Barlow command row without turning the lane's collapse into a one-man event or asserting his later brigadier rank.

records.name: John H. Worsham

sources.title: John H. Worsham
sources.author: National Park Service
sources.repository: Cedar Creek and Belle Grove National Historical Park
sources.note: Identifies Worsham as a Confederate soldier, says he enlisted in the 21st Virginia Infantry, fought at Antietam, and flags the later Cedar Creek looting/fatal-halt narrative.

sources.title: Pvt John H. Worsham
sources.author: Antietam on the Web
sources.note: Lists Worsham as a Confederate Private in the 21st Virginia Infantry, says he enlisted in Company F, and notes he was one of three Company F men on the Maryland Campaign.

sources.title: One of Jackson's Foot Cavalry
sources.author: John H. Worsham
sources.repository: Documenting the American South, University of North Carolina
sources.note: Participant memoir whose title identifies Worsham with Company F, 21st Regiment Virginia Infantry, Jackson's Division; used as a soldier-life source, not as a Lost Cause frame.

sources.title: Jones' Brigade
sources.author: Antietam on the Web
sources.note: Places the 21st Virginia Infantry in Jones' Brigade, Jackson's Division, Jackson's Command for the Sharpsburg Campaign.

sources.title: Antietam
sources.author: U.S. Army Center of Military History
sources.repository: U.S. Army Center of Military History
sources.locator: Order of battle, Jackson's Command / Jackson's Division / Jones' Brigade
sources.note: Official Army history order of battle places the 21st Virginia Infantry in Jones' Brigade under Jackson's Division at Antietam.

records.sourceNote: NPS identifies Worsham as a Confederate soldier in the 21st Virginia and says he fought at Antietam; Antietam on the Web verifies Private, Company F, and the Maryland Campaign. AotW/CMH place the 21st Virginia in Jones' Brigade, Jackson's Division. The record asserts no later sergeant/adjutant rank, no First Manassas claim, and no portrait asset.
records.bio: John H. Worsham served as a Confederate private in Company F, 21st Virginia Infantry. During the Maryland Campaign he was one of the few Company F men present, and the regiment belonged to Jones' Brigade in Jackson's Division at Antietam. His later memoir is useful as a soldier-life source, but this replacement keeps the claim narrow: an Antietam private in the 21st Virginia, not a later sergeant/adjutant rank or portrait claim.

records.name: Sullivan Ballou

sources.title: "My Very Dear Wife" - The Last Letter of Major Sullivan Ballou
sources.author: National Park Service
sources.note: Identifies Ballou as elected major in the 2nd Rhode Island Infantry, gives the July 14 letter context, and states he was killed as the Rhode Islanders advanced from Matthews Hill.

sources.title: Sullivan Ballou Papers
sources.author: Rhode Island Historical Society
sources.repository: Rhode Island Historical Society Manuscripts Division
sources.note: Verifies Ballou volunteered as Major in the Second Rhode Island, was wounded at Manassas/Bull Run, and documents the July 14 letters to Sarah, including copy-status caveat.

sources.title: Union Order of Battle - First Manassas
sources.author: National Park Service
sources.note: Places the 2nd Rhode Island in Burnside's Second Brigade under Hunter's Second Division, Army of Northeastern Virginia.

records.sourceNote: NPS identifies Ballou as elected major in the 2nd Rhode Island and killed as the Rhode Islanders advanced from Matthews Hill; RIHS confirms Major, Second Rhode Island, Bull Run wound/death, and the letter-copy caveat. NPS OOB places the regiment in Burnside's Brigade. The record asserts no company command, no autograph-original claim, and no portrait.
records.bio: Sullivan Ballou, a Providence lawyer and former Rhode Island House speaker, served as major in the 2nd Rhode Island Infantry at First Bull Run. Days before the battle he wrote Sarah a famous farewell letter from Camp Clark; the surviving archival trail includes copies rather than an unquestioned autograph original. At Manassas he was mortally wounded as the Rhode Islanders advanced from Matthews Hill. This replacement keeps the story narrow: a field officer of the 2nd Rhode Island in Burnside's Brigade, not a company officer or portrait claim.

records.name: Alexander S. Webb

sources.title: Union Commanders at Gettysburg
sources.author: National Park Service
sources.note: Identifies Webb as a newly appointed brigadier general placed in command of the Philadelphia Brigade and ties his troops to the Angle during Pickett's Charge.

sources.title: The Army of the Potomac at Gettysburg
sources.author: National Park Service
sources.locator: Second Corps / Second Division / Second Brigade (Philadelphia Brigade)
sources.note: Places Brig. Gen. Alexander S. Webb over the Philadelphia Brigade in Second Division, II Corps, with its 69th, 71st, 72nd, and 106th Pennsylvania regiments.

sources.title: Alexander Stewart Webb
sources.author: Congressional Medal of Honor Society
sources.locator: Recipient profile, Civil War, Gettysburg, July 3, 1863
sources.note: Verifies Webb's Brigadier General rank for the Medal of Honor action at Gettysburg on July 3, 1863 and the citation for leading his men at a critical period.

records.sourceNote: NPS identifies Webb as newly appointed brigadier general commanding the Philadelphia Brigade at Gettysburg and its troops throwing back the Angle breakthrough; NPS OOB places the brigade in Second Division, II Corps. CMOHS supports the July 3 action. No company command, no major-general-at-Gettysburg rank, no portrait.
records.bio: Alexander S. Webb commanded the Philadelphia Brigade at Gettysburg as a newly appointed brigadier general. On July 3, during Pickett's Charge, his brigade held the Union center near the Angle as Confederate troops reached the stone wall. The Medal of Honor citation later credited his personal gallantry in leading his men at a critical period. This replacement keeps the claim narrow: Webb as Philadelphia Brigade commander in II Corps, not a company officer, major general at Gettysburg, or portrait claim.

records.name: John O. Casler

sources.title: Four Years in the Stonewall Brigade
sources.author: John O. Casler
sources.repository: Internet Archive (1906 revised edition, Appeal Publishing Co.)
sources.note: Casler's own memoir places him at Chancellorsville with the 33rd Virginia — by his account detailed to the division Pioneer Corps, clearing felled timber from the Plank Road during the May 2 flank march and burying the dead afterward. First published 1893; revised and expanded 1906.

sources.title: Four Years in the Stonewall Brigade (American Civil War Classics)
sources.author: University of South Carolina Press
sources.locator: Book page with the Robert K. Krick introduction
sources.note: States Casler was a native of Gainesboro, Virginia, enlisted in June 1861 in Company A, 33rd Virginia Infantry, was captured in February 1865, and spent the war's final months imprisoned at Fort McHenry; confirms the 1893 first publication and 1906 revision.

sources.title: Order of Battle — Chancellorsville, Second Army Corps
sources.author: National Park Service
sources.locator: Confederate Second Corps order of battle
sources.note: Places the 33rd Virginia (Col. Abraham Spengler) in Paxton's Brigade (Stonewall Brigade), Trimble's Division under Brig. Gen. Raleigh Colston, Jackson's Second Corps, at Chancellorsville.

records.sourceNote: He did not witness Paxton's death; Jackson's wounding is secondhand; the pioneer-corps detail is his own account alone; Private rank is inferred from the absence of any attested promotion. USC Press confirms identity, Company A / 33rd Virginia, June 1861; the NPS OOB anchors the Stonewall Brigade / Colston chain. No portrait. Ratings stay neutral Inferred.
records.bio: John O. Casler served as a private in Company A, 33rd Virginia Infantry, Stonewall Brigade, and was at Chancellorsville with Paxton's Brigade of Trimble's Division, Colston commanding, in Jackson's Second Corps. By his own account he spent the battle detailed to the division Pioneer Corps, clearing felled timber from the Plank Road ahead of Jackson's May 2 flank column and burying the dead when the fighting ended. His memoir Four Years in the Stonewall Brigade (1893, revised 1906) — ranked by its University of South Carolina Press edition among the most sought-after and cited Confederate soldier accounts — is valued for its candor about straggling, foraging, and the unheroic labor of war.

records.name: Henry M. Stanley

sources.title: The Autobiography of Sir Henry Morton Stanley
sources.author: Henry M. Stanley (ed. Dorothy Stanley)
sources.note: Stanley's own account of enlisting in the Dixie Grays of the 6th Arkansas, marching to Shiloh, and being captured on April 6, 1862; a self-mythologizing memoir used only as a soldier-life source.

sources.title: Henry Morton Stanley Goes To War
sources.author: HistoryNet
sources.note: States Stanley marched as a private in the Dixie Grays, a volunteer company of the 6th Arkansas Infantry, on April 6, 1862 at Shiloh, was captured, and later took the Union oath as a 'galvanized Yankee.'

sources.title: Armies at the Battle of Shiloh
sources.author: J. Rickard
sources.locator: Confederate order of battle, Hardee's Third Corps, Hindman's First Brigade
sources.note: Places the 6th Arkansas Infantry (Col. A. T. Hawthorn) in Hindman's First Brigade (Col. R. G. Shaver commanding), Hardee's Third Corps, Army of the Mississippi, at Shiloh.

sources.title: 6th Arkansas Infantry Regiment
sources.author: Wikipedia contributors
sources.locator: Company organization; Company E, the 'Dixie Grays' of Arkansas County
sources.note: Identifies Company E of the 6th Arkansas as the 'Dixie Grays' of Arkansas County; corroborates the regiment's first combat at Shiloh.

records.sourceNote: Stanley's autobiography is self-mythologizing and is used only as a soldier-life source; no portrait is asserted and no claim is made about his contested later career. His private Dixie Grays (Co. E), 6th Arkansas service at Shiloh and his capture rest on the autobiography, HistoryNet, and the History of War order of battle. Ratings Inferred.
records.bio: Henry M. Stanley, born John Rowlands and later the African explorer Sir Henry Morton Stanley, enlisted in the Dixie Grays, Company E of the 6th Arkansas Infantry, and fought as a private at Shiloh on April 6, 1862. His regiment advanced with Hindman's brigade of Hardee's corps; Stanley was captured during the battle and, held at Camp Douglas, later took the Union oath as a 'galvanized Yankee.' This replacement uses only that narrow 1862 service, offering an enlisted Confederate's ground-level view of Shiloh and a reminder of how fluid loyalties could be, and makes no claim about his contested later career.

records.name: John Dooley

sources.title: Dictionary of Virginia Biography — John Edward Dooley (12 July 1842-8 May 1873)
sources.author: Library of Virginia
sources.repository: Library of Virginia, Dictionary of Virginia Biography
sources.locator: Biography entry; enlistment and promotion paragraphs
sources.note: States Dooley enlisted 11 August 1862 as a private in the 1st Virginia Infantry, was promoted first lieutenant on 10 April 1863, held a captaincy backdated to 17 May 1863, and was wounded in both thighs and captured during Pickett's Charge.

sources.title: Gettysburg: "The Whole Ground for Miles Around is Covered with the Wounded"
sources.author: John Dooley (American Battlefield Trust)
sources.note: Reproduces Lieutenant John Dooley's own Pickett's Charge account ('Shot through both thighs, I fall about 30 yards from the guns') and his capture as a Union prisoner.

sources.title: The Army of Northern Virginia at Gettysburg
sources.author: National Park Service
sources.locator: First Corps / Pickett's Division / Kemper's Brigade order of battle
sources.note: Places the 1st Virginia Infantry in Brig. Gen. James L. Kemper's Brigade, Maj. Gen. George E. Pickett's Division, at Gettysburg.

sources.title: Lt John E Dooley, First Virginia Infantry (1863)
sources.author: Antietam on the Web (Behind AotW)
sources.note: Corroborates Dooley's rank as a lieutenant of the 1st Virginia Infantry in 1863.

records.sourceNote: This record asserts Dooley's firm first-lieutenant rank, not the backdated 17 May 1863 captaincy or his 1862 private grade; Company C; no portrait; his journal is a participant account, not a Lost Cause frame. The DVB, the American Battlefield Trust (his own account), and the NPS OOB anchor the 1st Virginia / Kemper / Pickett chain and his capture. Ratings Inferred.
records.bio: John Dooley left his studies at Georgetown to enlist in the 1st Virginia Infantry, and by Gettysburg he was a lieutenant commanding Company C (the 'Montgomery Guard') in Kemper's Brigade, Pickett's Division. In Pickett's Charge on July 3, 1863, he was shot through both thighs about thirty yards from the Union guns, lay on the field overnight, and was captured. A battlefield captaincy was later backdated to May 17, 1863, so he is often styled captain at Gettysburg; this replacement uses his firm first-lieutenant rank and keeps the assault a shared, costly failure rather than a one-man feat. His postwar journal, published by Georgetown's Joseph T. Durkin, is read here as a participant account, not a Lost Cause frame.

records.name: Joseph H. De Castro

sources.title: Official Report for the 19th Massachusetts at Gettysburg
sources.author: Col. Arthur F. Devereux
sources.locator: After-action report listing captured Confederate colors; 'one by Corpl. Joseph H. De Castro'
sources.note: Col. Devereux's report names Corpl. Joseph H. De Castro among the captors of Confederate colors at the Angle on July 3, 1863; the primary report does not name the enemy regiment.

sources.title: The Army of the Potomac at Gettysburg
sources.author: National Park Service
sources.locator: II Corps / Second Division / Third Brigade (Hall) order of battle
sources.note: Places the 19th Massachusetts Infantry in Col. Norman J. Hall's Third Brigade, Gibbon's Second Division, II Corps.

sources.title: Joseph H. De Castro
sources.author: American Battlefield Trust
sources.note: Confirms De Castro as a color-bearer of the 19th Massachusetts bearing the Massachusetts state colors who captured a Confederate flag on July 3, 1863 and received the Medal of Honor; notes he later rose to sergeant.

sources.title: Hispanic-American Medal of Honor recipients
sources.author: The United States Army
sources.locator: Joseph H. De Castro entry; Company I, 19th Massachusetts; capture of the flag of the 19th Virginia, 3 July 1863
sources.note: U.S. Army article giving De Castro's Company I / 19th Massachusetts unit, the July 3, 1863 Gettysburg action capturing the 19th Virginia's flag, and his standing as widely credited first Hispanic-American Medal of Honor recipient.

records.sourceNote: The 19th Virginia identity rests on later secondary accounts, not Devereux's primary report; the 'first Hispanic-American recipient' framing is widely credited, not settled. No later sergeant rank and no portrait are asserted. Devereux's report names Corpl. De Castro; NPS OOB and the Battlefield Trust anchor the 19th Massachusetts / Hall / II Corps chain. Ratings Inferred.
records.bio: Joseph H. De Castro was a corporal and color-bearer in Company I, 19th Massachusetts Infantry, part of Col. Norman J. Hall's brigade at the Angle on July 3, 1863. During the repulse of Pickett's Charge he struck down an oncoming Confederate color-bearer with his own flagstaff, seized the enemy regimental colors -- identified by later accounts as those of the 19th Virginia -- and handed them to his colonel, Arthur F. Devereux. He received the Medal of Honor on December 1, 1864, and is widely credited as the first Hispanic-American recipient. This replacement keeps the deed inside the whole brigade's stand at Gettysburg and asserts his battle-day corporal rank, not his later sergeancy or a portrait.

records.name: Berry Benson

sources.title: Berry Benson (1843-1923)
sources.author: New Georgia Encyclopedia
sources.note: States Benson was named corporal of Company H, First South Carolina Regiment, campaigned in A. P. Hill's Light Division in 1862, and was crippled by a minie ball at Chancellorsville, causing him to miss Gettysburg.

sources.title: Confederate Sharpshooter Berry Benson and His Enfield
sources.author: American Rifleman (National Rifle Association)
sources.note: Places Benson in the 1st South Carolina, notes his selection for his brigade's sharpshooter work, states he was wounded at Chancellorsville and missed Gettysburg, and dates his promotion to sergeant to after his late-1864 return from prison.

sources.title: A. P. Hill's Light Division
sources.author: Wikipedia contributors
sources.locator: South Carolina brigade (Gregg-McGowan) and Chancellorsville sections
sources.note: Names the Gregg-McGowan Brigade (Samuel McGowan commanding after Maxcy Gregg's mortal wounding at Fredericksburg), lists the 1st South Carolina Infantry in that brigade, and places the division at Chancellorsville.

sources.title: Berry Benson's Civil War Book: Memoirs of a Confederate Scout and Sharpshooter
sources.author: Berry Benson (ed. Susan Williams Benson)
sources.locator: Memoir; 1st South Carolina / McGowan's Brigade service
sources.note: Benson's own memoir documents his 1st South Carolina service and his later fame as a Confederate scout and sharpshooter; used as a soldier-life source.

records.sourceNote: Rank at Chancellorsville is inferred as Corporal: NGE gives corporal at enlistment; American Rifleman dates his sergeant promotion to his late-1864 return from prison -- so May 1863 = corporal. No sharpshooter-battalion service is claimed at Chancellorsville (McGowan's battalion was organized in 1864); no portrait. NGE and American Rifleman confirm the Chancellorsville leg wound and missed Gettysburg; the Light Division page anchors the 1st South Carolina / McGowan chain (he was captured at Spotsylvania in May 1864). Ratings Inferred.
records.bio: Berry Greenwood Benson enlisted at eighteen as a corporal in Company H, 1st South Carolina Infantry, part of the Gregg-McGowan Brigade in A. P. Hill's Light Division. At Chancellorsville in May 1863 he was hit in the leg and crippled, and missed the Battle of Gettysburg while recovering at Augusta. He later became a celebrated Confederate scout and sharpshooter -- captured at Spotsylvania in May 1864, he famously tunneled out of the Elmira prison camp and was promoted to sergeant only after his late-1864 return. This replacement uses the narrow 1863 claim: a corporal of the 1st South Carolina in McGowan's Brigade, wounded at Chancellorsville, not a portrait or a formal sharpshooter-battalion rank at the battle.

records.name: John W. Green

team.corps: D. H. Hill's Corps (Polk's Right Wing)

sources.title: Johnny Green of the Orphan Brigade: The Journal of a Confederate Soldier
sources.author: John Williams Green (ed. Albert D. Kirwan)
sources.locator: Publisher page; enlisted-man journal, Shiloh through Atlanta
sources.note: Identifies John W. Green (1841-1920) as an enlisted man of Kentucky's Orphan Brigade who fought at Shiloh, Baton Rouge, Vicksburg, Chickamauga, and Atlanta, and whose journal gives the common soldier's perspective.

sources.title: Kentucky Confederate Veterans and Their War Records
sources.author: Genealogy Trails History Group
sources.note: States John W. Green, born 8 October 1841 in Henderson County, entered Confederate service 4 October 1861 and served as Sergeant-Major of the 9th Kentucky Infantry (the Orphan Brigade), under Generals Buckner, Breckinridge, Helm, and Lewis.

sources.title: Battle of Chickamauga order of battle: Confederate
sources.author: Wikipedia contributors
sources.locator: Helm's Brigade, Breckinridge's Division, D. H. Hill's Corps, Polk's Right Wing
sources.note: Lists the 9th Kentucky Infantry in Benjamin H. Helm's Brigade, Breckinridge's Division, D. H. Hill's Corps, within Polk's Right Wing at Chickamauga on 20 September 1863.

sources.title: Orphan Brigade
sources.author: Wikipedia contributors
sources.note: Names the 9th Kentucky among the Orphan Brigade's regiments and records that Brig. Gen. Benjamin H. Helm was mortally wounded leading the brigade at Chickamauga on 20 September 1863.

records.sourceNote: His exact grade at Chickamauga is not documented, but he is recorded rising to the regiment's Sergeant-Major (secondary accounts date a promotion at Shiloh, 1862, before Chickamauga), so the NCO slot takes his documented NCO track over a private baseline without asserting Sergeant-Major at the battle; company not identified; no portrait. UPK and Genealogy Trails anchor his 9th Kentucky / Orphan Brigade identity and rank; the Chickamauga OOB places Helm's Brigade in Breckinridge's Division, Polk's Wing. Ratings Inferred.
records.bio: John W. Green -- 'Johnny Green' -- was a Henderson County Kentuckian who entered Confederate service in October 1861 and served through the war as an enlisted man of the 9th Kentucky Infantry, one of the regiments of the Kentucky 'Orphan Brigade,' rising to the regiment's sergeant-major. At Chickamauga on September 20, 1863, his brigade -- under Brig. Gen. Benjamin Hardin Helm, in Breckinridge's Division of Polk's wing -- charged the Union breastworks on the LaFayette Road; Helm was killed in the assault. This replacement keeps the claim narrow: a noncommissioned officer of the 9th Kentucky whose journal, Johnny Green of the Orphan Brigade, is a valued common-soldier account, not a Sergeant-Major-at-the-battle or portrait claim.

records.name: William H. Tunnard

sources.title: William H. Tunnard
sources.author: Wikipedia contributors
sources.note: States Tunnard joined the 'Pelican Rifles,' which became a company of the 3rd Louisiana Volunteers; that as part of Company K he rose to sergeant; and that he was captured at Vicksburg on 4 July 1863 and paroled four days later; author of A Southern Record (1866).

sources.title: A Southern Record: The History of the Third Regiment Louisiana Infantry
sources.author: University of Arkansas Press
sources.locator: Publisher page (William L. Shea ed., 1997 reprint of the 1866 history)
sources.note: States Tunnard served in the Third Louisiana Infantry and offers an extensive first-person account of the regiment's capture and parole at the siege of Vicksburg.

sources.title: 3rd Louisiana Infantry Regiment
sources.author: Wikipedia contributors
sources.note: Places the 3rd Louisiana in Hebert's Brigade of Forney's Division at Vicksburg, records the Union mines exploded under the 3rd Louisiana Redan on 25 June and 1 July 1863, and cites Sergeant Tunnard's observations; the regiment was captured at the 4 July surrender.

sources.title: A Southern Record. The History of the Third Regiment, Louisiana Infantry. By W. H. Tunnard.
sources.author: William H. Tunnard
sources.repository: University of Michigan Library, Making of America
sources.locator: Digital edition ABJ7391.0001.001, Vicksburg siege chapters
sources.note: Tunnard's own 1866 regimental history and journal of the 3rd Louisiana, including its Vicksburg redan defense and the July 1863 surrender.

records.sourceNote: The record does not claim Tunnard was personally in a mine crater; it places him as a sergeant of the 3rd Louisiana, whose regiment held the 3rd Louisiana Redan; his Sergeant rank and Company K / Pelican Rifles origin rest on Wikipedia and his own regimental history; no portrait. The 3rd Louisiana Regiment page anchors the Hebert's Brigade / Forney's Division / redan chain and his 4 July 1863 capture at the surrender. Ratings Inferred.
records.bio: William H. Tunnard joined the Pelican Rifles of Baton Rouge at Louisiana's secession; the company became part of the 3rd Louisiana Infantry, and Tunnard rose to sergeant in Company K. During the siege of Vicksburg his regiment held the sector of the 3rd Louisiana Redan in Hebert's Brigade of Forney's Division -- the salient the Union twice mined and blew up, on June 25 and July 1, 1863 -- until Pemberton's garrison surrendered on July 4 and Tunnard was captured and paroled. He assembled the survivors' letters, diaries, and his own journal into A Southern Record (1866), long valued as a regimental history but read here as a participant/period account, not an uncritical authority. This replacement fills the Vicksburg Confederate soldier's-story slot without claiming Tunnard was personally in a mine crater or asserting a portrait.

records.name: Val C. Giles

team.army: Army of Tennessee (Longstreet's Corps, on loan from the Army of Northern Virginia)

sources.title: Rags and Hope: The Recollections of Val C. Giles, Four Years with Hood's Brigade, Fourth Texas Infantry, 1861-1865
sources.author: Valerius Cincinnatus Giles (Mary Lasswell, ed.)
sources.note: Giles's own memoir of four years in Company B, 4th Texas, Hood's Texas Brigade; recalls Chickamauga as 'the meanest, most unsatisfactory place I struck during the whole war.'

sources.title: Fourth Texas Infantry
sources.author: Texas State Historical Association
sources.note: The 4th Texas fought at Chickamauga on 19-20 September 1863 as part of Longstreet's First Corps, temporarily transferred to the Army of Tennessee, and helped break the Federal line on the second day.

sources.title: 4th Texas Infantry Regiment
sources.author: Wikipedia contributors
sources.note: Places the regiment in Robertson's Texas Brigade (3rd Arkansas, 1st/4th/5th Texas) at Chickamauga and quotes 'Sergeant Val Giles' on the second day's blue-jacket confusion, anchoring his sergeant rank at the battle.

sources.title: A Thousand Words a Battle: Chickamauga
sources.author: Emerging Civil War
sources.note: Places Robertson's Texas Brigade in Hood's Division of Longstreet's left wing at Chickamauga and describes its 20 September charge near the Brotherton Farm; brigade losses of 570.

records.sourceNote: Ships at Sergeant -- Giles's documented Chickamauga rank (4th Sergeant from May 1863; Wikipedia quotes 'Sergeant Val Giles' on the field); no portrait. His memoir Rags and Hope and TSHA place him in Company B ('Tom Green Rifles'), 4th Texas, Robertson's Texas Brigade of Hood's Division (Law commanding 20 Sep), Longstreet's Corps. Ratings Inferred.
records.bio: Valerius Cincinnatus 'Val' Giles enlisted at eighteen in the 'Tom Green Rifles,' Company B of the 4th Texas Infantry, and served through the war in Hood's Texas Brigade. Rising from private to 4th Corporal (November 1862) and 4th Sergeant (May 1863), he went west with Longstreet's Corps in September 1863 and fought at Chickamauga on 19-20 September in Robertson's Texas Brigade of Hood's Division -- the wing that broke the Federal line near the Brotherton Farm on the second day. Giles remembered Chickamauga as 'the meanest, most unsatisfactory place I struck during the whole war,' recalling the confusion when Longstreet's men were mistakenly issued blue Federal jackets. Captured at Wauhatchie on 29 October 1863, he later set down his recollections in Rags and Hope, a candid, unromantic soldier's memoir. This replacement fills a Chickamauga Confederate soldier's-story slot at his documented sergeant rank without asserting a portrait.

records.name: William Pitt Chambers

sources.title: Chambers (William Pitt) Diary, 1862-1865
sources.author: University of Southern Mississippi, McCain Library & Archives
sources.note: States Chambers was born 14 Dec 1839 in Covington County, a schoolteacher; enlisted 26 March 1862 with the 'Covington Rebels,' briefly the 6th Mississippi, then Company B, 46th Mississippi after October 1862; rose from private to first sergeant; at Vicksburg he was detailed to clerk for an adjutant but rejoined his company.

sources.title: Blood and Sacrifice: The Civil War Journal of a Confederate Soldier
sources.author: William Pitt Chambers
sources.note: The published edition of Chambers's journal ('My Journal'), documenting the 46th Mississippi from Shiloh through the siege of Vicksburg to the surrender at Citronelle.

sources.title: 46th Mississippi Infantry
sources.author: National Park Service
sources.note: States the 46th Mississippi was attached to Brig. Gen. William E. Baldwin's Brigade of Maj. Gen. Martin Luther Smith's Division during the Vicksburg campaign and siege.

sources.title: William Pitt Chambers (1839-1916)
sources.author: Find a Grave contributors
sources.note: Birth and death dates (1839-1916), corroborating the USM finding aid's biographical facts.

records.sourceNote: Does not claim the 46th held the Stockade Redan salient itself; it places Chambers as a first sergeant (shipped at Sergeant) of the 46th Mississippi in Baldwin's Brigade of M.L. Smith's Division, which held that northern sector; no portrait. Company B, rank, and Vicksburg service rest on the USM finding aid and NPS; journal Blood and Sacrifice. Ratings Inferred.
records.bio: William Pitt Chambers, a Covington County schoolteacher born in 1839, left home on 26 March 1862 with the 'Covington Rebels'; his company was briefly attached to the 6th Mississippi before becoming Company B of the 46th Mississippi Infantry after October 1862, in which he rose from private to first sergeant. At the siege of Vicksburg his regiment served in William E. Baldwin's Brigade of Martin Luther Smith's Division, holding the northern sector of the Confederate line; detailed to clerk for an adjutant, Chambers asked to rejoin his company and did picket duty and prepared its muster and pay rolls, often weakened by fever. He kept a daily diary he revised in 1891 as 'My Journal,' published in 1994 as Blood and Sacrifice -- a valued common-soldier record from Shiloh through Vicksburg to the war's end. This replacement fills the Vicksburg Confederate soldier's-story slot without claiming the 46th held the Stockade Redan salient itself or asserting a portrait.

records.name: Sam Houston Jr.

sources.title: Houston, Sam, Jr.
sources.author: Texas State Historical Association
sources.note: States Houston (b. 25 May 1843, d. 3 May 1894) served with the 2nd Texas Infantry, was wounded at Shiloh in April 1862, survived because a Bible in his pocket stopped a ball, and spent nearly six months as a prisoner including Camp Douglas before exchange.

sources.title: Sam Houston Jr.
sources.author: Wikipedia contributors
sources.note: States he enlisted August 1861 in Company C ('Bayland Guards'), 2nd Texas Infantry; was critically wounded and left for dead at Shiloh; a clergyman found his mother Margaret's Bible and letter; he was sent to Camp Douglas and exchanged; his father was removed as governor for refusing the Confederate loyalty oath.

sources.title: 2nd Texas Infantry Regiment
sources.author: Wikipedia contributors
sources.note: Places the 2nd Texas in J.K. Jackson's Third Brigade of Withers' Division, Bragg's Corps at Shiloh; the regiment stormed Stuart's camp and the Hornet's Nest on 6 April and was ambushed on 7 April, 'with Sam Houston Jr. among the wounded.'

sources.title: Second Texas Infantry
sources.author: Texas State Historical Association
sources.note: The regiment's own Handbook of Texas entry, corroborating its organization and Shiloh service under Col. John Creed Moore in Jackson's Brigade of Withers' Division, Bragg's Corps.

records.sourceNote: Ships at Private (his enlisted rank), placed for his documented 2nd Texas service -- not his famous father; wounded on Shiloh's second day (7 April 1862); no portrait. TSHA and Wikipedia anchor the Bible that stopped a ball, being left for dead, and his Camp Douglas captivity; the OOB is Jackson's Brigade, Withers' Division, Bragg's Corps. Ratings Inferred.
records.bio: Sam Houston Jr., eldest son of the Texas founder and Unionist governor Sam Houston -- removed from office for refusing to swear loyalty to the Confederacy -- enlisted in August 1861 as a private in the 'Bayland Guards,' Company C of the 2nd Texas Infantry. At Shiloh the regiment fought in John K. Jackson's Brigade of Withers' Division, Bragg's Corps, storming Stuart's camp and the Hornet's Nest on 6 April; on the second day, 7 April, it was ambushed and Houston was critically wounded and left for dead (some accounts place his wounding on the first day). A Bible in his pocket -- inscribed by his mother, Margaret -- stopped a second ball, and a Union clergyman who found the Bible and her letter had him carried off; he spent about six months a prisoner, part of it at Camp Douglas, before exchange and a medical discharge. This replacement fills a Shiloh Confederate soldier's-story slot at his enlisted private rank without asserting a portrait, echoing the war's fluid loyalties beside the Stanley record.

records.name: John S. Jackman

sources.title: Diary of a Confederate Soldier: John S. Jackman of the Orphan Brigade
sources.author: John S. Jackman; edited by William C. Davis
sources.note: Identifies Jackman's diary as an enlisted-man source from the Confederate Army of Tennessee's 9th Kentucky Brigade / Orphan Brigade.

sources.title: Jas. S. Jackman
sources.author: University of Louisville Libraries
sources.repository: Law Library of the Louis D. Brandeis School of Law, University of Louisville
sources.note: States Jackman enrolled in the Ninth Kentucky Infantry of the First Kentucky Brigade and fought at Shiloh, Vicksburg, and Chickamauga.

sources.title: The Atlanta Campaign: Dalton to Cassville, May 1-19, 1864
sources.author: David A. Powell
sources.locator: Excerpt p. 11 / PDF lines naming 'Pvt. John Jackman of Company B, the Confederate 9th Kentucky'
sources.note: Identifies Jackman as a private in Company B, Confederate 9th Kentucky, and cites the Davis edition of his diary.

sources.title: Chickamauga Campaign, Confederate Order of Battle
sources.author: Dave Powell / ACWScots
sources.note: Places the 9th Kentucky Infantry in Helm's Brigade, Breckinridge's Division, D. H. Hill's Corps at Chickamauga.

records.sourceNote: Ships at Private in the still-open private slot; Savas Beatie identifies Pvt. John Jackman of Co. B, Confederate 9th Kentucky; Louisville places him in the 9th Kentucky and at Shiloh, Vicksburg, and Chickamauga; the OOB places the regiment in Helm's Brigade/Breckinridge's Division. No portrait asset. Ratings Inferred.
records.bio: John S. Jackman, a Kentuckian born in 1841, served as a private in Company B of the Confederate 9th Kentucky Infantry, part of the Kentucky 'Orphan Brigade.' His diary, edited by William C. Davis, is one of the fullest enlisted-man accounts from the Army of Tennessee. At Chickamauga his regiment served in Helm's Brigade, Breckinridge's Division, on the Confederate right; Helm was mortally wounded during the assault on the Union breastworks near the LaFayette Road. This replacement fills the private slot beside the existing John W. Green NCO record, giving the same modeled division a second sourced enlisted voice without asserting a portrait or elevating Jackman above his documented private rank.

records.name: John Camden West

team.army: Army of Tennessee (Longstreet's Corps, on loan from the Army of Northern Virginia)

sources.title: West, John Camden, Jr.
sources.author: Vivian Elizabeth Smyrl
sources.note: Verifies West enlisted as a private in Company E, 4th Texas / Hood's Texas Brigade; reenlisted in April 1863 and fought at Gettysburg, Chickamauga, and Knoxville.

sources.title: A Texan in Search of a Fight
sources.author: John C. West
sources.locator: Title listing: diary and letters of a private soldier in Hood's Texas Brigade
sources.note: Catalogs West's own diary and letters as a private-soldier account in Hood's Texas Brigade.

sources.title: John C. West--A Texan in Search of a Fight
sources.author: John C. West
sources.repository: Daily Observations from The Civil War
sources.locator: Letter No. XVI, camp near Chattanooga, 24 September 1863
sources.note: Transcribes West's Chickamauga letter naming Company E casualties and describing his company's two days of close fighting.

sources.title: 4th Regiment, Texas Infantry
sources.author: National Park Service
sources.repository: Civil War Soldiers and Sailors System
sources.note: Places the 4th Texas in Hood's Texas Brigade and confirms it served with Longstreet at Chickamauga.

sources.title: Battle of Chickamauga order of battle: Confederate
sources.author: Wikipedia contributors
sources.locator: 20 September 1863, Longstreet's Corps, Hood's Division, Robertson's Brigade
sources.note: Places the 4th Texas in Robertson's Brigade, Hood's Division, with Evander M. Law commanding the division on 20 September 1863.

records.sourceNote: Ships at Private; TSHA verifies West reenlisted in Company E, 4th Texas and fought at Chickamauga; his diary title and Chickamauga letter keep this a private-soldier account; NPS/Wikipedia anchor the 4th Texas in Hood's/Robertson's brigade under Law's Division at Chickamauga. No portrait asset. Ratings Inferred.
records.bio: John Camden West, a Texas lawyer who gave up Confederate district-attorney appointments to see field service, reenlisted in Company E of the 4th Texas Infantry in April 1863. At Chickamauga his regiment served in Robertson's Texas Brigade, Hood's Division, with Evander Law commanding the division. His September 24 letter from near Chattanooga described two days of close woods fighting, Company E casualties, and a bayonet handle struck by a bullet after he removed it from his musket. This replacement fills the private slot beside Val C. Giles in the same modeled Law/Hood division without asserting a portrait, company command, or any rank above private.
